caption: Starting position -- the black King is in the center and must be driven to the edge
The Queen on e4 cuts the black King off from the bottom half of the board
Step 1: Queen on d5 creates a box -- the black King is confined to the top-right area
Step 2: White King advances to f3, maintaining the box with the Queen
Step 3: Shrink the box -- Queen moves to d7, pushing the King to the 8th rank
Step 4: Bring the King closer for the final checkmate
Checkmate! The Queen delivers the final blow on g7
STALEMATE! Black to move has no legal moves but is NOT in check -- the game is a draw
Another common stalemate trap -- the Queen is too close and takes away every square
Safe technique: place the Queen a Knight's move away from the enemy King, then bring your King up
Correct: Queen on g6 gives the King h8 as a legal square, while White's King approaches
Starting position -- the Rook cuts off the 5th rank, confining the black King
White King on e5 takes opposition against the black King on e7 -- the Rook is ready
After Kd7, White plays Ra7+ forcing the King to the 8th rank
The black King is pushed to the 8th rank -- checkmate is near
White takes opposition on d6, forcing Ke8, then Ra8# is checkmate
Step 1: Rook on a5 cuts off the 5th rank. Rook on b1 is ready to help.
Step 2: Rb6+ pushes the King to the 7th rank
Step 3: Ra7+ pushes the King to the 8th rank
Step 4: Rb8# is checkmate! The lawnmower is complete.
If the King approaches (Kd6), slide the Rook away: Ra5 moves to h5
A practical starting position with two Rooks vs King
Rooks on the 4th and 5th ranks create a wall -- the King is confined
Ra7+ forces the King to the back rank
Rh8# -- checkmate! One Rook checks, the other guards the 7th rank
Two Bishops on adjacent diagonals form a wall -- the black King is confined to the corner
Bishops form a diagonal barrier in the center -- the King is restricted to the upper-left area
The barrier tightens -- Bishops on b5 and c5 push the King toward the a8 corner
Checkmate! The King is trapped in the corner with no escape
White to move -- find checkmate in one move with the Queen
Solution: Qh7# -- a classic Queen checkmate on the edge
White to move -- what is the WRONG move here?
Correct: Qb7# delivers checkmate without stalemate
White to move -- deliver checkmate with the Rook
Solution: Ra8# -- the standard Rook checkmate pattern
White to move -- finish the lawnmower checkmate
Solution: Rh8# -- the lawnmower is complete
An empty board oriented correctly -- notice the light square in each player's bottom-right corner
The correct starting position with all 32 pieces placed
WRONG -- the King and Queen are swapped! The White Queen is on the dark square e1 instead of light square d1.
The Black King on e8 is in check from the White Queen on e5. Black must respond to this threat.
Checkmate! The Black King on h8 is attacked by the White Queen on h7. The King has no escape and no piece can help.
Back-rank checkmate -- the White Rook on e8 checkmates the Black King trapped behind its own pawns
Stalemate! It is Black's turn, but the Black King on a8 has no legal moves and is NOT in check. The game is a draw.
White's Knight on e5 blocks the Bishop on b2. If the Knight moves, the Bishop attacks the Rook on g7.
After Nf7! -- the Knight forks the King and Queen while the Bishop attacks the Rook. Black cannot deal with both threats.
A Rook on e1 is aimed at the Black King on e8, but White's own Bishop on e4 blocks the line.
The Bishop on d5 blocks the Rook on d1. Moving the Bishop to attack the Queen reveals the Rook's attack on d7.
The Knight on f3 blocks the Bishop on d1. Moving the Knight reveals the Bishop's diagonal toward h5.
The pawn on e5 blocks the Queen on e2. Pushing the pawn reveals the Queen's attack on e7.
White's Bishop on c1 is aimed at h6, but the Knight on e3 blocks... wait, the Rook on a4 is aimed at e4-e8! The Knight on e4 blocks the Rook's line to the Black King.
Classic discovered check: Bishop on b5 is blocked by Knight on d3. Moving the Knight reveals check.
White plays Nd6++ (double check). The Knight checks from d6 and the Bishop checks from c4. The King MUST move.
Double check leading to mate: After Nd6++ the King has no safe square. If Kf8, then Qf7#. If Kd7/Kd8, then Qe7# or Qd7#.
Anderssen's Immortal Game -- the final position. White has sacrificed massive material but delivers mate with minor pieces.
After Lasker's sacrifices, the Queen infiltrates via discovered lines. White's Rook and Queen coordinate to deliver the final blow.
Legal's Mate: After Black captures the Queen with Bxd1, White plays Bxf7+ Ke7, Nd5# -- a discovered attack leads to checkmate!

Puzzle 24: White to move. Find the double check that mates in one.
Puzzle 25: White to move. Triple threat -- discovered attack wins material and positional advantage.
Puzzle 26: White to move. A beautiful discovered attack combination wins material.
Puzzle 27: White to move. Discovered check leads to a mating attack.
Puzzle 28: White to move. Discovered attack with a quiet move wins.
Puzzle 29: White to move. Sacrifice and discovered double check leads to forced mate.
Puzzle 30: White to move. The ultimate discovered attack -- find the move that wins on the spot.
Classic Knight fork — the Knight on c7 attacks both the King on e8 and the Rook on a8
The family fork — Knight on d5 attacks the King, Queen, AND Rook at the same time
Before the pawn fork — White's pawn on d4 is ready to advance
After d4-d5! — the pawn forks the Knight on c5 and the Bishop on e5 (capture squares shown)
A devastating pawn fork — the e-pawn forks the Knight on d5 and the Bishop on f5
Queen fork — the Queen on a4 attacks both the King on e8 and the Rook on a8 along different lines
Another Queen fork — Qh5 attacks the uncastled King on e8 and the loose pawn on h7
Bishop fork — the Bishop on c6 attacks the Rook on a8 and the Knight on e4 simultaneously
Before — White sacrifices the Bishop on b5 to set up a Knight fork
After Bxd7+ Kxd7 — now the Knight can jump to c5, forking the King and Rook!
Absolute pin — the Bishop on g5 pins the Knight on f6 to the King on e7. The Knight cannot legally move.
Rook absolute pin — the Rook on e1 pins the Bishop on e5 to the King on e8
Relative pin — the Bishop on b5 pins the Knight on c6 to the Queen on d7
Relative pin along a file — the Rook on d1 pins the Knight on d4 to the Queen on d7
The Knight on f6 is pinned by the Bishop on g5. White plays Nd5 to add a second attacker.
The Knight on c6 is pinned to the Queen by Bb5. White plays a2-a4-a5 to attack the pinned Knight with a pawn.
Can you spot both a fork AND a pin in this position? (White to move)

The empty chess board — 64 squares in an 8x8 grid
The starting position — all 32 pieces in their correct squares
Pawn movement: forward one square, or two squares from starting position. Captures diagonally.
The Rook on d4 controls the entire d-file and the entire 4th rank
A Knight on d4 can move to 8 possible squares in an L-shape pattern
A Bishop on d4 controls both diagonals — it can reach any square on these lines
The Queen on d4 can move in 8 directions — combining Rook and Bishop movement
The King on e4 can move one square in any direction (8 possible squares)
The Italian Game after 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4
After 1.e4 e5 — both sides occupy the center
Giuoco Piano after 3...Bc5 — both Bishops eye the center
After 4.c3 Nf6 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 — White has the ideal pawn center
Giuoco Pianissimo after 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 Bc5 4.d3
A typical Pianissimo setup after 4.d3 Nf6 5.0-0 d6 6.c3 0-0
The Evans Gambit — 4.b4!? offering a pawn sacrifice
After 4...Bxb4 5.c3 Ba5 6.d4 — White has a powerful center and open lines
Two Knights Defense after 3...Nf6 — Black counterattacks e4
After 4.Ng5 d5 5.exd5 Na5 — the main line of the Two Knights
Legal's Mate — the final position after 8.Nd5# (checkmate)
A typical ideal Italian Game setup for White — pieces are fully developed and coordinated
White to play — should White push d4 here?
White to play in a Pianissimo structure — what is the best plan?
Black to play — White just moved Ng5. What should Black do?
White pawn on a5. The "square" stretches from a5 to a8 to e8 to e5. Black King on g7 is outside the square — the pawn promotes!
Now the Black King is on d7 — inside the square. If it is Black's turn, the King catches the pawn.
Direct opposition on the e-file. If it is Black to move, White has the opposition — Black must step aside.
Opposition in action: White King on e5 and pawn on e4 vs Black King on e7. White to move loses the opposition; Black to move lets White break through.
Pawn on e4: the key squares are d6, e6, and f6 (two ranks ahead). If the White King reaches any of these squares, the pawn promotes.
Pawn on e5: now the key squares shift to d6, e6, f6, d7, e7, and f7.
A winning position: White King on d6 ahead of the pawn. The King controls the key squares, and the pawn will promote.
A drawn position: White King behind the pawn on e1, pawn on e4, Black King on e6. Black maintains the opposition and draws.
White's d5 pawn is a passed pawn — no Black pawns on the c, d, or e files can stop it. The e5 pawn is NOT passed because Black has a pawn on f7.
Connected passed pawns on c5 and d5 supported by the White King. These two pawns together are very difficult to stop.
The classic breakthrough position. White has pawns on a5, b5, c5 vs Black pawns on a7, b7, c7. White to move wins!
White's a-pawn is an outside passed pawn. Black must chase it with the King, and White's King then feasts on Black's kingside pawns.
White needs to triangulate. From d4, White plays Kd3-e3-d4, returning to d4 but with Black to move.
Classic zugzwang: Black to move must step aside, letting the White King advance. If White were to move, it would be White in zugzwang.
Mutual zugzwang: whoever has to move here is at a disadvantage. Both sides want to pass!
Both sides have a passed pawn. Use the Rule of the Square to determine who wins the race!
Black is down a pawn but can draw by taking the opposition. Black to move: ...Ke7! directly facing the White King.
White wins by advancing the a-pawn to lure the Black King away, then capturing on the kingside.
The Queen's Gambit position after 1.d4 d5 2.c4 — White offers the c-pawn
White's c4 pawn attacks the d5 square, creating central tension
White's ideal classical center with pawns on d4 and e4
Typical QGD pawn structure — Black's e6 pawn is solid but locks in the light-squared bishop
The Orthodox Defense after 6...Nbd7 — a rock-solid setup for Black
The Ragozin Defense after 4...Bb4 — Black pins the knight and fights for the center
The Tartakower Variation after 7...b6 — Black prepares Bb7 to activate the problem piece
Queen's Gambit Accepted after 2...dxc4 — Black takes the gambit pawn
Classical QGA after 6...a6 — Black has returned the pawn and prepares queenside expansion
Modern approach with 3.e4 — White boldly claims the center
White executing a minority attack — the b-pawn advances to create queenside weaknesses
What variation is this? What should Black play next?
